title: Road to Compliance: Will Your Internal Users Hate Your Platform Team?
gist: Sevdesk engineering manager Davide de Paolis describes how the accounting-software company's second platform team recovered from a predecessor team's burnout-driven collapse (caused by over-engineering around AWS vendor lock-in fears) by adopting native AWS services and rolling out a 'minimum viable governance' tagging/compliance program using a staged inform-then-enforce methodology built on AWS Organizations, Security Hub, SCPs, and an automated EventBridge/Lambda Slack-notification pipeline.

themes.theme: How the first platform team failed

insights.claim: Sevdesk's original platform team burned out and was dissolved after fear of AWS vendor lock-in led them to build self-hosted alternatives (Keycloak for access management, Rancher for a Kubernetes UI, Longhorn for block storage, Harbor as an image repository) on top of EKS instead of using native AWS services, consuming all their maintenance capacity and leaving no time to build the actual platform roadmap.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A cautionary case study: over-indexing on avoiding vendor lock-in can itself become the resource sink that kills a platform team, a risk pattern relevant to any organization evaluating build-vs-buy for infrastructure tooling.
insights.locator: The Sevdesk Platform Engineering Team (Foundational Work)

insights.claim: The successor platform team replaced self-hosted tools with AWS-native equivalents (IAM/SSO for Keycloak, EBS for Longhorn, ECR plus Inspector for Harbor, plus Security Hub, AWS Config, and GuardDuty), which pleased security via native integration but pushed migration work (pipeline changes, new skills) onto product teams, generating friction and complaints about degraded developer experience.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: Illustrates that platform teams frequently underestimate the migration burden they transfer to consuming teams, even when the underlying technical decision (native services) is sound.
insights.quote: That's a shitty developer experience. It was so easy before, like pushing to production or doing whatever I wanted. Why do I need to do that? Now it sucks.
insights.locator: We had to answer all these comments

insights.claim: A multi-account AWS Organizations setup (split by environment, team, and domain) was the first foundational change, chosen for data/access isolation, tailored security controls per environment, per-account service-quota isolation, and clean per-team cost allocation.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A standard, reusable foundational pattern (multi-account AWS Organizations) that should precede any serious platform-team compliance or governance program.
insights.locator: First thing, we moved to AWS organization

insights.claim: AWS provides separable enforcement mechanisms -- informational tag policies, hard-guardrail Service Control Policies (SCPs), and detection via Security Hub/resource tagging standards -- that can each be activated independently, letting a team run an inform-first, enforce-later rollout instead of breaking deployments overnight.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A reusable, staged rollout playbook for internal cloud-governance initiatives that avoids the all-or-nothing enforcement failure mode that killed the prior team.
insights.locator: AWS has a lot of tools that are natively integrated

insights.claim: Service Control Policies hit hard platform limits in practice -- a 5,000-character policy length cap and a cap on the number of SCPs allowed per account/organizational unit -- forcing the team to split enforcement into separate policies for foundational resources, general applications, and serverless.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A concrete technical constraint (SCP length/count limits) that any team designing AWS-wide guardrails needs to plan around from the start.
insights.locator: we started building this service control policy

insights.claim: The team pivoted from trying to enforce compliance on every resource type to a 'minimum viable governance' approach, using the SQL-over-cloud-API tool Steampipe to identify only the top cost/usage 'heavy hitter' services and focusing enforcement exclusively there.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A prioritization heuristic (impact/effort-driven, not exhaustive) directly applicable to any team facing a large backlog of compliance or security findings (the talk cites 12,000 Security Hub findings as an example of an intractable full-scope approach).
insights.locator: let's change approach and focus on the heavy hitters

insights.claim: When the team activated AWS's resource tagging standard in Security Hub purely to measure compliance before enforcing anything, they discovered a large share of non-compliant resources belonged to their own platform team, validating the decision to delay hard enforcement and reinforcing the importance of a platform team leading by example.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A concrete lesson that governance teams should audit themselves before enforcing rules company-wide, or risk credibility loss.
insights.locator: We let Security Hub run for a day. The next day, how many resources were not compliant? A lot.

insights.claim: The team built an automated ownership-inference and notification pipeline: EventBridge detects resource creation/change events missing an owner tag, a Lambda function looks up ownership from CI/CD pipeline metadata rather than the unreliable AWS 'created-by' tag (which under infrastructure-as-code just reflects the CI runner's assumed IAM role, not the actual team), batches events through a queue to avoid Slack notification spam, and posts weekly per-team compliance scorecards via Amazon Q (formerly AWS Chatbot) to Slack.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A concrete, technically detailed, reusable automation pattern for tag/ownership compliance nudging that other platform teams can replicate directly, including the specific caveat that CI/CD-deployed resources render AWS's default 'created-by' tag useless for ownership attribution.
insights.locator: Relying on AWS config, which is something that we already added

insights.claim: The informed-soft-enforce-enforce rollout methodology is described as reusable 'rinse and repeat' for any internal compliance initiative regardless of company size, with only the underlying automation scripts needing to change per initiative.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A generalized playbook applicable well beyond tagging -- to security-vulnerability remediation, cost governance, or any other internal platform-driven compliance push.
insights.quote: This process of informing, soft enforcing, and then enforcement can be applied for every internal compliance initiative. Rinse and repeat, change maybe the scripts, the concept still proves true and works within a company of any size.

insights.claim: Externally-mandated regulatory or audit-driven compliance deadlines get significantly more organizational support than purely internal platform-team-driven governance, because they mobilize legal, finance, and security simultaneously, giving the initiative multi-department backing that a platform team acting alone cannot generate.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: Explains why internal governance initiatives are structurally harder to push through than externally mandated compliance (e.g. EU regulation) -- relevant context for platform teams assessing why some compliance pushes stall while audit-driven ones move fast.
insights.locator: In this example, I'm referring to internal compliance and internal governance

insights.claim: The team deliberately deferred adopting an internal developer platform tool like Backstage as premature, choosing to prioritize narrower wins (tagging, security vulnerability remediation, EKS workload migration) first rather than over-scoping the platform investment.
insights.speaker: Davide de Paolis
insights.so_what: A sequencing lesson against premature adoption of heavyweight internal-developer-platform tooling before foundational governance work is done.
insights.locator: No, we haven't chosen to roll our own. We evaluated Backstage.
